summary_L1_0_04590da1.text: The Company's financial performance is significantly influenced by seasonal holiday demand, historically leading to higher first-quarter net sales, and by new product and service introductions, which impact net sales, cost of sales, operating expenses, and indirect channel inventory as products are launched or anticipated. Products gross margin and its percentage increased in both Q1 2021 compared to Q1 2020 (driven by higher volume, different product mix, and leverage) and in Q1 2022 compared to Q1 2021 (due to a different product mix and foreign currency strength). Similarly, Services gross margin and its percentage rose in both Q1 2021 and Q1 2022 compared to prior years, primarily due to higher net sales, a different Services mix, and increased leverage, partially offset by higher Services costs. Despite these improvements, the Company anticipates future gross margins will experience volatility and downward pressure. Concurrently, both Research and Development and Selling, General and Administrative expenses increased in Q1 2021 compared to Q1 2020, mainly driven by higher headcount-related expenses, with R&D investments highlighted as critical for the Company's future growth and competitive position.

summary_L1_1_43c664f9.text: These financial disclosures provide a detailed overview of the Company's performance and financial position, primarily for the three months ended December 26, 2020, and December 28, 2019, and certain balance sheet details as of September 26, 2020. The included notes detail computations for basic and diluted earnings per share, disaggregated net sales by significant products, services, and categories, along with condensed consolidated financial statement details, other income and expense, and changes in accrued warranties and related costs. Comprehensive income is also addressed, specifically foreign currency translation adjustments, derivative instruments, marketable debt securities, and reclassifications from accumulated other comprehensive income (AOCI) by component. A significant portion describes the Company's reportable segments—Americas, Europe, Greater China, Japan, and Rest of Asia Pacific—managed on a geographic basis using a "management" approach, detailing their evaluation based on net sales and operating income, and providing reconciliation to consolidated statements. All monetary figures are typically presented in millions, while share counts are in thousands.

summary_L1_2_eeca4d23.text: Apple Inc.'s condensed consolidated financial statements, prepared in accordance with U.S. GAAP, consolidate all wholly-owned subsidiaries, eliminate intercompany transactions, and rely on management's significant estimates and assumptions, noting that actual results may materially differ. The Company operates on a 52- or 53-week fiscal year ending the last Saturday of September. Revenue, derived from products like iPhone, Mac, and iPad, and from Services, is generally recognized when control of goods or services transfers to the customer (e.g., upon product shipment or over time for service delivery). For multi-performance obligation arrangements, revenue is allocated using Stand-alone Selling Prices (SSPs); delivered hardware is recognized immediately, while bundled services (such as iCloud) and future software upgrades are deferred and recognized straight-line over their expected provision period. The Company accounts for third-party App Store sales on a net commission basis and manages its iPhone Upgrade Program as a guarantee liability. Key operational considerations include reliance on single or limited sources for critical components, with hardware manufacturing primarily outsourced to Asia, and the ongoing, uncertain impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on global economic activity, retail operations (leading to temporary store closures), and supply chains, despite the Company's belief in its sufficient liquidity. Management's Discussion and Analysis section also highlights forward-looking statements regarding these inherent risks and uncertainties.

summary_L1_3_e217c6e7.text: The Company manages its debt through two primary instruments: unsecured short-term promissory notes ("Commercial Paper") and longer-term notes ("Notes"). Commercial Paper, used for general corporate purposes including dividends and share repurchases, typically matures in less than twelve months; outstanding amounts fluctuated from $5.0 billion at December 26, 2020, to $1.7 billion by December 31, 2022, and $2.0 billion at December 30, 2023, with a weighted-average interest rate of 0.10% at December 26, 2020. The Company's Term Debt consists of senior unsecured floating- and fixed-rate Notes, which had an aggregate principal/carrying amount of $105.9 billion at December 26, 2020, increasing to $117.8 billion by December 25, 2021, and subsequently decreasing to $109.4 billion by December 31, 2022. The fair value of these Notes was $117.2 billion at December 26, 2020, and $98.0 billion at December 31, 2022, while interest cost on term debt totaled $628 million for the three months ended December 26, 2020. To manage interest rate and foreign currency risks associated with its Notes, the Company utilizes interest rate and foreign currency swaps, which are incorporated into the effective interest rates.

summary_L1_4_e94a12ae.text: The Company demonstrated strong financial performance across two consecutive fiscal first quarters, with net sales increasing 11% ($12.5 billion) in Q1 2022, primarily driven by growth in iPhone, Services, and Mac. This followed an impressive 21% ($19.6 billion) increase in Q1 2021, fueled by higher net sales across all Products and Services categories and all geographic segments. Key product launches included the M1 Pro/Max MacBook Pro, third-generation AirPods, and Apple Watch Series 7 in Q1 2022, building on Q1 2021 introductions such as the iPhone 12 series, M1-powered Macs, and Apple Fitness+. While iPhone, Mac, and Wearables, Home and Accessories (including AirPods, Apple Watch, and HomePod) consistently contributed to sales increases, iPad net sales experienced a decrease in Q1 2022 due to lower sales of the 10-inch version. Revenue recognition for both Products and Services includes the amortization of deferred values for bundled items; specifically, products include software upgrade rights, while services encompass bundled offerings like Maps, Siri, free iCloud storage, and Apple TV+. Conversions from deferred revenue contributed $3.0 billion in Q1 2022 (from September 2021) and $2.5 billion in Q1 2021 (from September 2020), with total deferred revenue standing at $12.4 billion as of December 2021, 64% of which is expected to be recognized within a year. All geographic segments, including Americas, Europe, Greater China (benefiting from a stronger renminbi), Japan (aided by a stronger yen), and Rest of Asia Pacific, reported increased net sales. The Company also returned significant capital to shareholders, repurchasing $20.4 billion of common stock in Q1 2022 and $24.0 billion in Q1 2021, alongside dividend payments of $3.7 billion and $3.6 billion, respectively.

summary_L1_5_2b9cf9b9.text: As of December 26, 2020, the Company's financial position included **$11.6 billion in total deferred revenue**, up from $10.2 billion on September 26, 2020, with 64% of this amount anticipated to be realized within one year, 26% within one-to-two years, 8% within two-to-three years, and the remaining 2% over three years. A substantial portion of its financial instruments, **$19.5 billion in marketable securities** ($18.6 billion as of September 26, 2020), was restricted from general use, primarily due to the **European Commission's State Aid Decision**. This decision involved an adjusted recovery amount of **€12.9 billion plus €1.2 billion in interest**, which was funded into escrow pending the conclusion of ongoing legal proceedings. The Company's investment policy for its highly-rated, investment-grade securities emphasizes minimizing principal loss, with non-current debt maturities typically ranging from one to five years. Additionally, the Company held **non-marketable equity securities with a carrying value of $2.5 billion** ($2.8 billion as of September 26, 2020) and restricted cash mainly supporting its iPhone Upgrade Program. Its considerable trade receivables were diversified, with cellular network carriers accounting for 41% as of December 26, 2020, and credit risk was managed through various strategies, including credit insurance and third-party financing. However, vendor non-trade receivables exhibited concentration, with two vendors representing 61% and 14% of the total at year-end. During the three months ended December 26, 2020, operating activities generated **$38.8 billion in cash**, while **$8.6 billion was used in investing activities** (primarily for marketable securities and property, plant, and equipment) and **$32.2 billion in financing activities**, largely driven by **$24.8 billion in common stock repurchases** and **$3.6 billion in dividends**. Despite significant cash outflows, the Company asserts its current liquidity, encompassing cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities, is sufficient to meet its operational and financial requirements for the next 12 months.

summary_L1_6_d9048ee4.text: The Company strategically employs derivative financial instruments, including forward contracts, options, and swaps, and occasionally non-derivative instruments, to mitigate business exposure to foreign currency exchange and interest rate risks. These derivatives hedge forecasted cash flows (e.g., revenue and inventory purchases, typically for up to 12 months), net investments in foreign subsidiaries, and existing foreign currency-denominated term debt or marketable securities, with recognition periods extending up to 22 years for FX and 7 years for interest rate hedges as of December 26, 2020. Derivatives are categorized as Cash Flow, Net Investment, or Fair Value hedges, impacting Accumulated Other Comprehensive Income (AOCI), Other Comprehensive Income (OCI), or earnings, respectively, with non-designated derivatives fair valued through earnings. Notably, fair value hedge amounts excluded from effectiveness assessments generated gains of $82 million (Q4 2020) and $128 million (Q4 2019) recognized in Other Income/(Expense), net, while other excluded effectiveness assessment amounts recognized in OCI were losses of $138 million (Q4 2020) and $89 million (Q4 2019). All derivatives are recorded at fair value using Level 2 inputs, and associated cash flows are treated as operating activities; credit risk is managed through master netting arrangements and collateral security, resulting in net cash collateral posted of $215 million as of December 26, 2020, and potential netting effects reducing gross derivative assets and liabilities to net derivative liabilities of $684 million and $312 million as of December 26, 2020, and September 26, 2020, respectively.

summary_L1_7_f670757d.text: The Company maintained an active capital return program, primarily through share repurchases and dividends. Under its share repurchase program, which allows for privately negotiated and/or open market transactions including those compliant with Rule 10b5-1 and does not obligate the Company to acquire a specific number of shares, authorization levels increased year over year. As of December 26, 2020, the Company had utilized $192.6 billion of a $225 billion authorized program, with $32.4 billion remaining, having repurchased 200 million shares for $24.0 billion in the fourth quarter of 2020. By December 25, 2021, the authorization expanded to $315 billion, with $274.5 billion utilized and $40.5 billion available, as the Company repurchased 124 million shares for $20.4 billion during the fourth quarter of 2021, a figure which included 30 million shares initially delivered through new $6.0 billion accelerated share repurchase (ASR) agreements with financial institutions, whose final share count and average price are to be determined by the volume-weighted average price until Q2 2022. Concurrently, the Company's quarterly cash dividend increased from $0.205 per share as of December 26, 2020, to $0.22 per share by December 25, 2021, with an annual increase intended. Additionally, Section 16 officers, including Timothy D. Cook, Katherine L. Adams, Luca Maestri, Deirdre O'Brien, and Jeffrey Williams, maintained their own Rule 10b5-1 trading plans during both periods, pre-establishing future stock transactions.

summary_L1_8_9d7cb12b.text: The Company implemented a four-for-one stock split on August 28, 2020, retroactively adjusting all related share and per-share data, and also adopted the new ASU 2016-13 standard for credit losses at the start of Q1 2021, which had no material impact on its financial statements. As of December 26, 2020, 713 million shares were reserved for future issuance under stock plans, which include Restricted Stock Units (RSUs) that generally vest over four years; the fair value of RSUs at vesting reached $8.5 billion for the three months ended December 26, 2020, significantly up from $4.2 billion in the prior year. Unrecognized compensation costs related to outstanding RSUs and stock options totaled $18.2 billion as of the same date, expected to be recognized over a weighted-average period of 2.9 years. Concurrently, the Company reported gross unrecognized tax benefits of $17.1 billion as of December 26, 2020, with $9.0 billion potentially impacting the effective tax rate, and $1.6 billion in accrued interest and penalties; however, a decrease of up to $4.1 billion in these benefits is considered reasonably possible within the next 12 months, though tax years after 2014 remain open in certain foreign jurisdictions. Furthermore, Other Income/(Expense), Net, decreased in Q1 2021 compared to Q1 2020, primarily driven by lower interest income and a non-marketable security adjustment, reflected in a decline of the weighted-average interest rate earned on cash and equivalents from 2.08% to 1.49%.

summary_L1_9_fde8c4f9.text: The Company faces substantial financial and legal contingencies, beginning with $8.1 billion in off-balance sheet unconditional purchase obligations as of December 26, 2020, primarily for content, internet, and supplier services. Beyond these commitments, the Company is engaged in numerous significant legal proceedings and regulatory challenges. These include patent infringement lawsuits by VirnetX and Optis, where juries awarded damages of $503 million and $506 million respectively, both of which the Company is challenging; VirnetX's patents were also declared invalid by the PTO. In consumer protection matters, the Company reached settlements totaling up to $500 million in U.S. federal and state class actions and $113 million with multi-state attorneys general concerning iOS performance management cases, though it maintains its devices were not defective. Major regulatory actions include a €1.1 billion fine from the French Competition Authority over sales practices, which the Company has appealed. The high-profile Epic Games antitrust lawsuit against the App Store largely favored the Company on antitrust claims but resulted in a U.S. Supreme Court-upheld injunction requiring the Company to permit external links for purchasing, a compliance plan Epic is disputing. Additionally, the Masimo patent dispute led to an ITC-issued import ban on certain Apple Watch models, which was resolved when a Company redesign was approved by CBP. The Company is also under intense global regulatory scrutiny, particularly from the European Union's Digital Markets Act (DMA), facing multiple non-compliance investigations that could result in fines up to 10% of its annual worldwide net sales and necessitate further App Store business changes. These ongoing legal and regulatory matters, including other antitrust investigations and potential impacts on commercial relationships, pose significant risks to the Company's business, financial condition, and reputation.

summary_L2_0_11977ea2.text: The Company manages its financial obligations primarily through unsecured short-term Commercial Paper (ranging from $1.7 billion to $5.0 billion between 2020 and 2023, with a 0.10% weighted-average interest rate in 2020) and longer-term senior unsecured Notes, which saw their aggregate principal fluctuate from $105.9 billion in December 2020 to $109.4 billion by December 2022, carrying a fair value of $98.0 billion at the latter date and incurring $628 million in interest cost for Q4 2020. To mitigate foreign currency exchange and interest rate risks inherent in its debt and other financial positions, the Company strategically employs derivative financial instruments, including forward contracts, options, and swaps. These derivatives are designated as Cash Flow, Net Investment, or Fair Value hedges, covering forecasted cash flows, foreign subsidiary investments, and foreign currency debt, with effectiveness periods up to 22 years for FX and 7 years for interest rate hedges. All derivatives are fair valued monthly using Level 2 inputs, with associated cash flows recognized as operating activities; credit risk is managed via master netting arrangements and collateral, leading to net derivative liabilities of $684 million by December 2020, partly offset by $215 million in net cash collateral posted.

summary_L2_1_6e785cda.text: As of December 26, 2020, the Company reported a robust financial position, marked by $11.6 billion in total deferred revenue (with 64% expected within a year) and $19.5 billion in marketable securities, the latter largely restricted due to an escrowed €12.9 billion plus €1.2 billion interest related to the European Commission's State Aid Decision. Operating activities generated $38.8 billion in cash during the quarter, largely funding $24.8 billion in common stock repurchases and $3.6 billion in dividends, while $8.6 billion was used for investing activities, confirming the Company's assessment of sufficient liquidity for the next 12 months. Despite this strong financial footing, the Company faces significant financial and legal contingencies, including $8.1 billion in off-balance sheet purchase obligations and ongoing legal battles such as patent infringement suits by VirnetX ($503 million) and Optis ($506 million) which are being challenged, as well as consumer protection settlements totaling up to $500 million for iOS performance management. Major regulatory challenges include a €1.1 billion fine from the French Competition Authority (under appeal), the Epic Games antitrust lawsuit which, while largely favoring the Company, resulted in an injunction requiring external purchase links, and a resolved Apple Watch import ban due to a Masimo patent dispute. Further, the Company is under intense global regulatory scrutiny, particularly from the EU's Digital Markets Act, with non-compliance investigations potentially leading to fines up to 10% of annual worldwide net sales and necessitating significant changes to its App Store business model.

summary_L2_2_5be6dc97.text: The Company demonstrated strong financial performance across two consecutive first quarters, with net sales increasing 21% ($19.6 billion) in Q1 2021 and 11% ($12.5 billion) in Q1 2022, primarily driven by new product introductions like the iPhone 12 series, M1 Macs, M1 Pro/Max MacBook Pro, and Apple Watch Series 7, alongside robust Services growth across all geographic segments, though iPad net sales decreased in Q1 2022. Both Products and Services gross margins improved during these periods, attributable to factors such as higher volume, favorable product/service mix, and foreign currency strength, despite anticipated future volatility and downward pressure. Operating expenses, including Research and Development and Selling, General and Administrative, increased due to higher headcount. The Company recognized $2.5 billion (Q1 2021) and $3.0 billion (Q1 2022) from deferred revenue conversions, contributing to a total deferred revenue of $12.4 billion as of December 2021, while also returning significant capital to shareholders through $24.0 billion (Q1 2021) and $20.4 billion (Q1 2022) in stock repurchases and consistent dividend payments.

summary_L2_3_ff40b0bb.text: The Company actively managed an expanding capital return program, primarily through substantial share repurchases and an increasing cash dividend, while Section 16 officers also maintained Rule 10b5-1 trading plans. The share repurchase authorization significantly grew year-over-year, from $225 billion in 2020 to $315 billion by December 25, 2021. As of December 26, 2020, the Company had utilized $192.6 billion, with $32.4 billion remaining, after repurchasing 200 million shares for $24.0 billion in the fourth quarter of 2020. By December 25, 2021, $274.5 billion had been utilized with $40.5 billion available, including 124 million shares repurchased for $20.4 billion during the fourth quarter of 2021, of which 30 million shares originated from new $6.0 billion accelerated share repurchase (ASR) agreements. Simultaneously, the quarterly cash dividend increased from $0.205 per share as of December 26, 2020, to $0.22 per share by December 25, 2021, with an annual increase intended. Additionally, Section 16 officers, including Timothy D. Cook, maintained their own Rule 10b5-1 trading plans for pre-established future stock transactions during both periods.

summary_L2_4_f522a571.text: These financial disclosures by Apple Inc. provide a detailed overview of its performance and financial position for the three months ended December 26, 2020, and December 28, 2019, alongside balance sheet details as of September 26, 2020, all prepared in accordance with U.S. GAAP and consolidating wholly-owned subsidiaries, with the Company's 52- or 53-week fiscal year ending the last Saturday of September. Revenue is generally recognized when control transfers, with multi-performance obligations allocated using Stand-alone Selling Prices (SSPs), leading to immediate recognition for hardware but deferred, straight-line recognition for bundled services (e.g., iCloud) and future software upgrades, while App Store sales are on a net commission basis and the iPhone Upgrade Program is a guarantee liability. The Company manages operations through geographic reportable segments—Americas, Europe, Greater China, Japan, and Rest of Asia Pacific—evaluated based on net sales and operating income, with all monetary figures typically presented in millions and share counts in thousands. Key operational considerations and risks, highlighted in management's discussion, include reliance on single or limited component sources, outsourced hardware manufacturing primarily in Asia, and the ongoing, uncertain impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on global economic activity, retail, and supply chains, despite management's belief in its sufficient liquidity; these disclosures inherently involve management's significant estimates and assumptions where actual results may materially differ.
